does: This paper proposes a data curation framework that neutralizes harmful content in LLM training datasets through an iterative text revision process. The method reduces perplexity of potentially malicious texts as perceived by language models while preserving semantic quality, operating without prior knowledge of specific attack mechanisms. The framework addresses two vulnerability scenarios: integration of harmful content in pre-training datasets and direct tampering through fine-tuning. By applying this curation to both pre-training and fine-tuning phases, the approach aims to degrade the effectiveness of jailbreak attempts while maintaining model utility and safety alignment across downstream tasks.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about LLM safety alignment and adversarial robustness in the era of large-scale crowdsourced training data. It extends prior research on data poisoning attacks against neural networks and safety fine-tuning methods by focusing specifically on preventive data curation rather than reactive defense mechanisms. The paper connects to literature on instruction-following vulnerabilities, alignment drift during fine-tuning, and the brittleness of RLHF-based safety approaches. It also relates to broader data quality and filtering work, such as efforts to remove toxic content from training corpora, but distinguishes itself by framing this as an adversarial robustness problem where adversaries actively inject harmful content into training pipelines.
enables: This framework opens avenues for more robust safety practices in LLM deployment and training pipelines. It could enable organizations to implement data curation as a standard preprocessing step, reducing reliance solely on post-hoc alignment techniques. The approach points toward future work on certified defenses against data poisoning, potentially combined with anomaly detection to identify suspicious texts. It may facilitate development of automated systems for continuous dataset monitoring and cleaning in production environments. Additionally, the method could inspire research into understanding which text properties make models vulnerable to jailbreaking, leading to deeper insights into safety alignment mechanisms and more principled approaches to building inherently robust foundation models.
